metaDescription: Learn what Base64 does, where to use it, why it makes data bigger, and why it should never be mistaken for encryption.

quick.what: Turns binary data into text-safe characters.
quick.use: Sending files or bytes through systems that only handle plain text well.
quick.notFor: Protecting secrets. Anyone can decode Base64 in seconds.

intro.title: In plain English
intro.text: Base64 is a packaging format. It takes raw bytes and rewrites them using letters, numbers, plus, slash, and equals so the result can travel safely through text-first systems.

how.title: How it works
how.text: Base64 groups data into chunks of 3 bytes, then remaps those 24 bits into 4 printable characters. That is why the output looks different but is still fully reversible.

useCases.title: Where you'd use it
items: Embedding images or files inside JSON or API payloads.
Sending binary data through email, form posts, or other text-only channels.
Creating data URLs for small assets in HTML or CSS.

mistake.title: Common mistake
mistake.text: People often assume Base64 hides data because the output looks like gibberish. It does not. If someone can read the string, they can usually decode it immediately.

history.title: History / fun fact
history.text: Base64 became popular because older systems and protocols were much better at handling text than raw binary. The tradeoff is size: the output grows by roughly one third.

security.title: Security note
security.text: If you need confidentiality, encrypt first and only use Base64 afterward as a transport wrapper.

sections.title: Why the output gets larger
sections.text: Three original bytes become four Base64 characters. That extra character count makes the result around 33% larger than the original data.

sections.title: Why padding appears
sections.text: When the input length is not divisible by 3, Base64 adds '=' padding so the output still fits the expected 4-character grouping.

diagram.title: Three bytes become four characters
diagram.caption: Base64 reshapes the same bits into a text-safe alphabet. It changes representation, not meaning.
